title: Chapter 4 Master Quiz
description: Test your mastery of if, else, switch, and decision making.

questions.question: What happens if the condition in an 'if' statement is false?
questions.explanation: If the condition evaluates to false, JavaScript completely skips over the associated { } code block.

questions.question: Which of the following is written correctly?
questions.explanation: The correct grammar is: the keyword 'if', followed by the condition in parentheses (), followed by the code block in curly braces {}.

questions.question: The 'else' statement requires a condition in parentheses, like else (x === 5).
questions.explanation: The 'else' statement acts as a default catch-all. It has no condition of its own—it simply runs if all preceding checks failed.

questions.question: In an if / else if / else chain, how many blocks of code will actually be executed?
options: All of them that are true
questions.explanation: JavaScript guarantees that only the first block to evaluate to true will run. The rest of the chain is instantly skipped.

questions.question: Which statement acts as the universal backup plan in an if-chain?
questions.explanation: The 'else' statement handles anything that didn't match the previous conditions.

questions.question: If you have an `else if` for `score >= 80` and another for `score >= 90`, which MUST come first to grade a 95 correctly?
questions.explanation: JavaScript executes top-to-bottom. If >= 80 comes first, a 95 will trigger it and the code will report a B instead of checking for the A!

questions.question: What evaluates to true? if(" ")
questions.explanation: " " is a string with a space character inside it. It is Truthy, so the if block would execute.

questions.question: It is bad practice to nest an 'if' statement inside another 'if' statement (more than once or twice).
questions.explanation: Deeply nesting if statements creates the 'Pyramid of Doom'. It's much better to use Logical Operators (&& / ||) to combine the conditions!

questions.question: What is the cleanest way to check if age is OVER 18 AND has a ticket?
questions.explanation: Using the Logical AND (&&) flattens out nested logic into a single, clean 'if' statement.

questions.question: Which of these is incorrect code?
questions.explanation: You cannot shortcut conditions. '|| "Tue"' simply checks if the string "Tue" is truthy. You MUST restate the full check: day === "Tue".

questions.question: When should you use a 'switch' statement instead of 'if/else'?
options: When checking if a number is greater than another number
When comparing exactly one variable against many specific values
When you only have two options
When you are using boolean variables
questions.explanation: Switch statements are designed to elegantly route a single exact value (like a menu selection or days of the week) into many possible paths.

questions.question: What happens if you forget to include a 'break;' in a switch case?
options: The code ignores that case completely
It executes that case's code AND also the code in the cases directly below it
It treats the case like a comment
questions.explanation: Forgetting 'break' causes 'fall-through'. The code inside your matching case runs, but then JavaScript keeps blindly running the contents of the cases below!

questions.question: Switch statements under the hood perform loose equality comparisons (==).
questions.explanation: Switch statements use strict equality (===), meaning the values must match in both content AND data type.

questions.question: The _____ keyword at the end of a switch statement functions exactly like an 'else' block.
questions.explanation: The 'default' keyword provides a catch-all block just in case none of the other cases matched.

questions.question: What happens if the condition is `if(0)`?
questions.explanation: 0 is falsy, so the if block is skipped entirely.

questions.question: Which structure should wrap the condition directly?
questions.explanation: The condition block goes within (), immediately following the `if` keyword.

questions.question: Are ternary statements recommended for long, multi-path logic?
questions.explanation: While they save space, deeply nested ternaries are extremely difficult to read. They should only be used for simple, binary decisions.

questions.question: What represents the execution block below a 'case' in a switch?
options: The code directly following the colon (:)
questions.explanation: In a switch, code executes sequentially following the colon (:) of the matched case until a break is encountered.

questions.question: Why would `if(password = "admin")` be a serious bug?
options: It uses the assignment operator, changing the password to 'admin'
questions.explanation: Using a single = inside an if statement assigns the value instead of comparing it. It assigns 'admin' to password, which evaluates as Truthy, bypassing security.

questions.question: An `if` statement must ALWAYS be followed by an `else` statement.
questions.explanation: An 'if' block can stand completely on its own! 'else' is totally optional.

questions.question: What is the best substitution for a massive, 20-condition `else if` block based on one variable?
questions.explanation: Switch statements are designed specifically to handle checking many specific values against a single target cleanly.

questions.question: Is `if(true && "false")` Truthy or Falsy?
questions.explanation: The string "false" is Truthy. So (true && Truthy string) evaluates to Truthy.
